article_title: I wanted a simpler Brave alternative, and this new browser surprised me
core_thesis: Helium is a privacy-focused, open-source browser built on Ungoogled Chromium that strips away bloat and telemetry, offering a minimal yet functional alternative to Brave. It prioritizes essential features like built-in uBlock Origin, split-view, and local bangs, while deliberately omitting a password manager and DRM support to maintain simplicity and security. Despite being in beta with some limitations, it provides a focused browsing experience that respects user privacy without asking for anything in return.
detailed_summary: The article describes the author's search for a simpler alternative to Brave, which led to Helium, an open-source browser developed by a two-person team. Helium is built on Ungoogled Chromium, removing Google's background services and data-sharing hooks. Its setup wizard is minimal, allowing users to choose a default search engine (DuckDuckGo by default), import bookmarks and extensions, and toggle privacy features. The new tab page is intentionally blank with an 'Add shortcut' button, and the toolbar shows only essential icons by default, with options to add more like split-view. Split-view enables side-by-side browsing within a single window, useful for research. Helium ships with uBlock Origin preinstalled and active, addressing concerns about Google's Manifest V3 transition that degrades ad blockers in standard Chrome. It also features a 'bangs' system (over 10,000 shortcuts) that lets users jump directly to websites via address bar shortcuts, processed locally for privacy. The browser deliberately omits a built-in password manager, arguing passwords should not live inside the browser; it recommends using dedicated password managers like Bitwarden or KeePassXC. However, Helium is still in beta: DRM support is missing (Netflix, Prime Video won't work), automatic updates are only on macOS (Windows/Linux users must manually update from GitHub), and there is no native sync between devices. The author concludes that despite these caveats, Helium is a focused, privacy-respecting tool that asks nothing from the user, making it worth the switch for those frustrated with feature-bloated browsers.

entities.name: Helium
entities.role: The open-source browser being reviewed, built by a two-person team.

entities.name: Brave
entities.role: The browser the author was using before switching to Helium; described as feature-rich but bloated.

entities.name: Ungoogled Chromium
entities.role: The stripped-down Chromium base that Helium is built on, removing Google services.

entities.name: uBlock Origin
entities.role: Ad blocker preinstalled in Helium, effective against Manifest V3 limitations.

entities.name: Oluwademilade Afolabi
entities.role: Author of the article, tech enthusiast and writer for MakeUseOf.

key_data_points: Helium is built on Ungoogled Chromium, which removes Google's background services and data-sharing hooks, ensuring no telemetry or Google tracking (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: reduces attack surface and data leakage).

Helium ships with uBlock Origin preinstalled and active, which is critical because Google's Manifest V3 transition degrades ad blocker effectiveness in standard Chrome (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: ad blockers can be used to block malicious domains and trackers during reconnaissance).
Helium proxies extension requests through its own servers so Google cannot log what extensions you install (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: prevents Google from profiling your toolset).

Helium deliberately omits a built-in password manager, arguing passwords should not live inside the browser; it recommends using dedicated password managers like Bitwarden or KeePassXC (🟢 relevant to your Cybersecurity: aligns with best practices for credential security).

Helium features over 10,000 bangs (shortcuts) that allow direct navigation to websites via address bar, processed locally for privacy (🟢 relevant to your productivity: speeds up workflow for research and development).

Helium is still in beta: DRM support is missing (Netflix, Prime Video won't work), automatic updates are only on macOS, and there is no native sync between devices (🟢 relevant to your homelab: may require manual updates and alternative sync solutions like Nextcloud).
Helium's split-view feature allows two websites side by side in one window, useful for research and note-taking (🟢 relevant to your student needs: enhances productivity for studying and coding).
methodology_steps: Download Helium from its official website or GitHub repository.
Run the setup wizard: choose a default search engine (DuckDuckGo recommended), import bookmarks and extensions from your current browser, and toggle privacy features.
Optionally, customize the toolbar by going to Settings > Appearance > Toolbar to add buttons like split-view.
To use split-view, right-click any link and select 'Open in Split View', or enable the toolbar button first.
Use bangs by typing shortcuts in the address bar (e.g., '!w banana' for Wikipedia, '!gh' for GitHub). The full list is at Helium.computer/bangs.
If you want to self-host Helium's proxy services for extension updates and filter downloads, use the open-source code on GitHub.
For password management, install a dedicated password manager extension like Bitwarden or KeePassXC, and export/import your passwords from your previous browser.
On Windows or Linux, manually check for updates on GitHub periodically, as automatic updates are only available on macOS.
conclusions: Helium offers a genuinely minimal and privacy-focused browsing experience that rivals Brave, with the added benefit of being built on Ungoogled Chromium and including uBlock Origin out of the box. Its deliberate omissions (no password manager, no DRM, no sync) align with a philosophy of doing less but doing it right, which may appeal to users who want a browser that doesn't demand anything from them.

The browser's bangs feature and split-view are practical productivity enhancements that work locally, preserving privacy. These features are particularly useful for research-heavy workflows, such as studying or coding, where quick navigation and side-by-side content are beneficial.

Helium's beta status means it has limitations (no DRM, manual updates on Windows/Linux, no sync), but these are acceptable trade-offs for users who prioritize privacy and simplicity. The author concludes that Helium is worth the switch for those frustrated with feature-bloated browsers like Brave, Chrome, or Edge.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your interest in privacy-focused tools and lightweight software for your homelab and daily use. As a programmer and cybersecurity enthusiast, Helium's Ungoogled Chromium base and built-in uBlock Origin provide a secure, ad-free browsing environment that reduces tracking and attack surface. The bangs feature can speed up your workflow when accessing GitHub, documentation, or AI tools like ChatGPT. The split-view is useful for side-by-side coding and research. However, note the lack of DRM and sync; you may need to keep another browser for streaming and use a syncing solution like Nextcloud for bookmarks. The article also reinforces good security practices by recommending external password managers over browser-stored passwords.
extra_advice: Consider setting up Helium as your secondary browser for development and research tasks, while keeping a DRM-capable browser (like Firefox or Brave) for media. You can also experiment with self-hosting Helium's proxy services on your homelab server to gain full control over extension updates and filter downloads, further enhancing privacy and reducing reliance on third-party infrastructure.
